overview: Enable operators to open an interactive terminal session from the console Pods view and execute commands inside the active loop container, with auditable attach/detach/command events and live output in the pod detail journal panel.
goals: Allow an operator to attach from the web console to the running container for a selected active loop
Allow line-based command execution from the pod detail view with output streamed back into the terminal panel
Preserve security and auditability for every attach, command, and detach action
Keep the feature aligned with existing loop state and journal streaming architecture
nonGoals: Full byte-level PTY emulation (for example curses/full-screen TUIs)
Cross-cluster pod exec beyond the cluster where smith-api is running
Collaborative multi-cursor terminal editing between multiple operators
successMetrics: From pod detail page, attach succeeds within 5 seconds p95 when target loop pod is Running
Command round-trip (submit to first output line in journal) is under 1 second p95 for simple commands like 'echo ok'
100% of attach/detach/command actions create both audit records and journal events with actor metadata
Unauthorized terminal control attempts are rejected with HTTP 401/403 and execute zero pod commands
At least one Playwright scenario validates attach, command, output visibility, and detach from console pod detail
openQuestions: Should only one operator be allowed to issue commands per loop at a time, or can multiple attached operators issue commands concurrently?
What should the default idle timeout be before automatic detach (for example 5, 10, or 15 minutes)?
Which shell fallback order should be used when the container does not provide /bin/bash (for example /bin/sh only)?
Should command output be truncated per command and, if yes, what byte limit is acceptable?
Is this feature restricted to pods started by smith-core only, or also manually created debug pods with matching labels?

stack.framework: Existing static console (console/index.html with vanilla JavaScript) and Go HTTP API (cmd/smith-api)
stack.hosting: Kubernetes via Helm (smith-console and smith-api services)
stack.auth: Existing bearer token operator auth in smith-api plus actor attribution in request payloads

routes.name: Pods Grid
routes.purpose: List loops as pod tiles and open selected loop detail

routes.name: Pod Detail Terminal
routes.purpose: Show live journal and interactive command controls for the selected loop

routes.name: Attach Terminal Session
routes.purpose: Validate loop runtime is attachable and register operator attachment

routes.name: Execute Terminal Command
routes.purpose: Run command in active loop container and journal command output

routes.name: Detach Terminal Session
routes.purpose: Terminate operator terminal session and record audit trail

routes.name: Loop Runtime Resolution
routes.purpose: Return resolved namespace/pod/container and attachability status for console UX

uiNotes: Pod detail page keeps existing live journal panel and adds a command composer row (single-line input + Run button + keyboard submit).
Attach button state machine: disabled when loop not active, enabled when active and attachable, loading during attach, attached state after success.
Command composer states: disabled before attach, enabled after attach, busy while command in-flight, error banner on API failure.
Surface resolved runtime target metadata (namespace/pod/container) in pod detail header for operator clarity.
Retain existing reconnect behavior for /journal/stream and render command output lines with visible operator-command prefix.
On mobile widths, composer wraps without hiding journal output; primary actions remain keyboard accessible.

importFormat.description: Not applicable for this feature; no external import payload is introduced.

rules: This is an existing codebase; all work must extend current API and console flows without re-platforming.
Attach is allowed only when loop state is active and a matching runtime pod/container is resolvable and Running.
Command execution requires prior attach by the same actor; otherwise return conflict and do not exec in pod.
Each successful command execution appends structured journal entries for command start and command result with exit code.
Detach is idempotent from UI perspective: first detach succeeds, repeated detach returns conflict but does not mutate runtime.
Terminal session auto-detaches when loop reaches terminal state or runtime pod is no longer available.
Command payloads exceeding defined max length are rejected with HTTP 400 and logged as rejected actions.
All control endpoints remain authorization-protected through existing bearer token checks.

stories.title: Resolve runtime pod/container for a loop
stories.description: As an operator, I want the API to resolve the current runtime pod/container for a loop so the console can determine whether terminal attach is possible.
acceptanceCriteria: Add runtime resolution logic that maps loop ID to namespace, pod name, container name, and attachability status.
Expose `GET /v1/loops/{id}/runtime` with explicit fields for `attachable` and human-readable `reason` when false.
Example: for an active loop with Running replica pod, endpoint returns `attachable=true` with concrete pod and container names.
Negative case: for a terminal loop or missing pod, endpoint returns `attachable=false` with reason `loop not active` or `runtime pod not found`.
Unit tests cover running, pending, terminal, and missing-runtime scenarios.

stories.title: Attach and detach terminal sessions against runtime target
stories.description: As an operator, I want attach/detach to represent a real session bound to the resolved runtime target so command execution is controlled and auditable.
acceptanceCriteria: Update `POST /v1/loops/{id}/control/attach` to require runtime resolution success and persist session metadata including runtime target reference.
Update `POST /v1/loops/{id}/control/detach` to close only existing actor attachment and append corresponding audit/journal records.
Example: attach from `terminal=console-pods` on a running loop returns status `attached` and increments attach count for the actor.
Negative case: attach attempt when runtime pod is not Running returns HTTP 409 and does not create attached session state.
Attach/detach tests assert audit metadata includes actor, terminal source, and runtime target identity.

stories.title: Execute line commands inside active loop container
stories.description: As an attached operator, I want `control/command` to execute inside the loop container and stream results into the loop journal so I can interact from console.
acceptanceCriteria: Implement pod exec integration using existing Kubernetes client-go dependencies (`k8s.io/client-go/tools/remotecommand`) without introducing a new service.
Run submitted commands via shell wrapper (for example `/bin/sh -lc`) and capture stdout/stderr plus exit status.
Set command response `delivered=true` when exec is attempted and include exit code/result metadata.
Example: command `echo hello` appends journal output containing `hello` and returns successful exit code.
Negative case: actor submits command without attach and receives HTTP 409 with no pod exec call performed.
Negative case: command over max size returns HTTP 400 with validation error and audit entry tagged as rejected.

stories.title: Add interactive command controls to pod detail UI
stories.description: As an operator, I want a command input in the pod detail page so I can attach and run terminal commands directly from the console.
acceptanceCriteria: Route `#pod-view/:loopID` includes explicit components: attach button, detach button, command input, run button, status indicator, and runtime target summary.
UI states are defined and implemented: `idle`, `attaching`, `attached`, `executing`, `detaching`, and `error`.
Command submission supports Enter key and Run button, disables controls while executing, and clears input only on success.
Example: user opens a running loop in pod detail, clicks attach, runs `pwd`, and sees command output appear in journal panel.
Negative case: user opens non-active loop; attach and command controls remain disabled with visible reason message from runtime endpoint.
Playwright test validates route behavior and control state transitions on success and failure paths.

stories.title: Harden security and operational limits for web terminal control
stories.description: As a platform owner, I want secure and bounded terminal control so console access cannot execute unauthorized or abusive command traffic.
acceptanceCriteria: All attach/command/detach handlers enforce existing authorization and reject unauthenticated calls before runtime resolution.
Add per-session command rate limiting and max command length validation with explicit API error codes.
Audit records include whether actions were accepted or rejected and include rejection reason when blocked.
Example: authorized operator issues valid command within limits and receives accepted execution path.
Negative case: unauthorized request (missing/invalid bearer token) receives HTTP 401 and produces no command execution in runtime pod.
Negative case: rapid burst exceeding rate policy returns throttling response and records throttle reason in audit metadata.

stories.title: Verification coverage and operator documentation
stories.description: As a maintainer, I want automated coverage and docs for console terminal control so the feature is reliable and operable in Kubernetes environments.
acceptanceCriteria: Add/extend API unit tests for runtime resolution, attach/detach lifecycle, command validation, and execution result handling.
Add integration-style tests (or harness tests) covering real or simulated Kubernetes exec flow for successful and failing commands.
Add Playwright coverage for pods page to pod detail attach -> command -> output visible -> detach flow.
Update docs/loop-ingress-and-cli.md and console-facing docs with API contracts, required permissions, and troubleshooting steps.
Example: test suite demonstrates attach plus `echo ok` command flow and terminal output visibility in UI artifacts.
Negative case: documented troubleshooting includes pod missing/not-running scenario and expected console/API error surfaces.
